description:
You are a data-scientist who just received a very noisy list of people’s first-names.
    The list already exists on the machine at:

        /home/user/datasets/raw_names.txt

    Your goal is to produce a clean, de-duplicated frequency table that will later be
    imported into a downstream analytics pipeline.

    Cleaning rules
    1. Treat the file as UTF-8 plain text, one name per line.
    2. For every line:
       • Trim all leading / trailing white-space characters.  
       • Convert the entire line to lower-case.  
       • Discard the line if, after trimming, it is empty.
    3. After cleaning, count how many times each distinct name appears.
    4. Sort the resulting <name, count> pairs
       • primarily by descending count,  
       • secondarily (when counts are equal) by ascending alphabetical order of the name.

    Output artefacts you must create
    1. The frequency table must be written to

           /home/user/output/name_frequency.tsv

       Format requirements that will be automatically tested:
       • UTF-8 text, Unix line endings.  
       • Each record on exactly one line.  
       • Two fields per line, **separated by a single tab character** (<TAB>):  
           <count><TAB><name>  
       • No leading or trailing white-space on any line.  
       • **Do not print any header row.**  
       • The file must end with a single newline character.  
       • Example of the correct structural format (values are illustrative):

             23	alex
             17	maria
             17	nicole
              9	zoe

    2. A concise, plain-text log of the commands you used during the cleaning-and-sorting
       procedure, saved to

           /home/user/output/command_log.txt

       Log format:
       • One command per line, exactly as you typed it in the shell.  
       • Include only the commands that directly contribute to producing the
         frequency table (omit navigation commands such as “cd”).  
       • Ensure the file is non-empty; it will be spot-checked by automated tests.

    Additional requirements & constraints
    • Work only with standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities that are guaranteed
      to exist in an Alpine-based container (e.g. cat, tr, sed, awk, sort, uniq, cut, tee, etc.).  
    • You do **not** need root privileges.  
    • Create any missing intermediate directories yourself; do **not** modify or
      rename the original raw_names.txt file.  
    • You should be able to accomplish this task with roughly 5-10 commands.
    • Verify that the final /home/user/output/name_frequency.tsv file exactly
      follows the format rules before you finish.
truth:
Initial files present before the task starts
    -------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/datasets/raw_names.txt
       Contents (19 lines, including intentional casing and
       trailing/leading spaces on some lines):

           Alice
           bob
           Charlie
           alice
           Bob
           alice   
           Alice
           charlie
           Bob
           Dana
           Eve
           eve
           Eve
           alice
           bob
           dana
           Eve
           Alice
           charlie

    No /home/user/output directory exists initially.

    Expected artefacts after successful completion
    ----------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/output/name_frequency.tsv

       Exact expected contents (including the trailing newline at the very end):

           6	alice
           4	bob
           4	eve
           3	charlie
           2	dana

    2. /home/user/output/command_log.txt
       • Must exist.  
       • Must contain at least one non-blank line.  
       • No exact content is enforced by tests beyond non-emptiness.

    Directory & permission notes
    ----------------------------
    • The /home/user directory tree is writable by the agent’s user.  
    • The testing framework will verify both the presence and byte-perfect content
      of /home/user/output/name_frequency.tsv, and that
      /home/user/output/command_log.txt exists and is not empty.
